Personally, I find the first exam to be hard and really push me to think for a solution. One thing that I noticed is that it's really hard for me to understand the question at first, as to what the requirements are and the inputs are, they're too vague for me to understand and got me a hard time starting the solution I could think of. But for an engineer really prepped for a coding assessment like this they would have done it better, given that the expectation for the exam is communicated to the candidate.

Rest API part of the exam is doable also, I just run out of time reading and assessing the problem requirements and solution. There's just a slight misunderstanding on how the API endpoint is working. It's really hard to visualize the resource and the endpoint is returning, what parameters does it accept and what not. Also, the expected output seemed misleading as the endpoint I was expecting I can query the result, but the expectation was to gather all the data from all pages and extract the data for the specific requirements needed which I think is really not the best practice for typical RESTful API endpoint. But for exam technicality it provides the candidate to think outside the box. I can say that it is doable.

With the react, I find this exam to be easy and very straight forward for a React Engineer. As for the real-life application if we we're to pick a candidate I think it's best to use an actual endpoint for displaying and managing data as a result. This way a better understanding if the candidate really worked on a project before because that's how a react application typically work, Consuming an API endpoint to fetch and modify data to the servers.

Overall, the exam asses the engineer to think critically and know their capabilities in both Ruby, Ruby on Rails, and React.

During the AI round, the available programming language options were limited, which created a barrier for candidates not proficient in those specific languages. Expanding the options to include languages such as Python, Swift, TypeScript, and Java would make the round more inclusive.
Current rounds appeared to be tailored specifically for candidates skilled in Spring Boot & React (or Node.js & Angular), leaving little room for those with equivalent experience in other tech stacks.
As a result, a significant portion of time in the AI round was spent deciphering unfamiliar syntax rather than demonstrating actual problem-solving ability, which does not accurately reflect a candidate's true technical competence.

Couple of things:
1. The intellisense on the last coding problem stopped working and it cost me around 10 minutes to get it working again.
2. The inability to debug properly by placing break points and inspecting values is a problem, since putting console.writeline or printf statements into the code is time consuming, and does not reflect the true developer experience.
3. When my intellisense failed, I tried to use ALT+SPACE to try and trigger it, which is normal for any editor. However, myorganisation has the Copilot app installed on my windows machine, and its bound to the ALT+SPACE hot key, which in turn had the copilot chat window pop up. This can cause false positives with regards to irregularities.
4. On the final coding question I got, it was not clear if I should be randomly reorganising the array according to all possible combinations, or whether it would be passed in. The examples never showed reorganisation as part of the algorithm to be performed, except on the first step. It says 'the mamimum is 7', but fails to show an example of which combination actually makes up 7, so this is incredibly confusing. I think that question needs to be relooked at to ensure the participant understands what is excepted more clearly.

I think HackerRank isn't the most suitable platform for hosting lengthy challenges like this one. The UX isn't really designed for sessions that require you to stay focused for extended periods — it can feel fatiguing rather than engaging. A platform with a more comfortable editing experience and better support for long-form problem solving would be a better fit for this type of challenge.

n my opinion, the test is adequate for evaluating a Java and Spring Boot developer. Part 1, which focuses on core Java, is a good exercise. The allotted time is sufficient to complete the solution, and the problem can be approached either through core Java implementation or by using Streams, which helps assess the candidate’s depth of expertise.

For Part 2, the Spring Boot section, I was unfortunately unable to fully focus due to personal matters. That aside, I think it is a well-designed exercise that effectively evaluates a candidate’s understanding of the required architecture and their ability to correctly implement and configure the necessary beans. The time provided should also be enough for candidates to deliver a working solution.

One comment regarding the IDE: while the AI assistance can be helpful, it can also be confusing at times. I also found it somewhat challenging to navigate the project structure. I’m not sure if there was a walkthrough of the workspace features, but if there wasn’t, providing a brief guide or orientation would be beneficial for future candidates.

To Whom It May Concern,

Thank you so much for the opportunity. I really appreciate it.

But unfortunately, the experience with your technical assessment was awful. I started the test from the React question. And the IDE was so lagging. Each interaction made a huge page freeze for 5 - 10 seconds.

Yeah, 10 - 20 minutes I spent planning, thinking, and understanding the project structure and code. But the rest of the time ~ 1h 10m - 1h 20m, I spent fighting with page freezing. It really was such a horrible experience.

And I had an issue with the browser console. I have opened it to check the income data, and I couldn't close it again.

If talking about the React question in general, I like it. It shows the real-world issue, not some generic algorithm-type question that you will never use it in your work.
